speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'd like to go back just for a minute. You were talking during the break about, just as the war was starting and the Russians came and took everything away and your father lost his business.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's lost his business and he was looking for a job. He became a night watchman. A night watchman-- he got ill. He got sick. And I, as a young boy-- somebody had to-- I took his place for a few days, a few nights actually until he got well. And it wasn't easy but somebody had to do it. And, you know, and, it was very little pay. I saw it in the house. It was a mess. I mean, that's why I wait a little bit later on, I went to the school, you know, to get away from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war started, what were your thoughts of what you were going to be when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea, no idea. I was, uh, under, I was seven. I was about 14 maybe, 14 and 1/2 years old, you know, when the war-- when, huh, 1939. When I've just finished the seven grades. What idea I have? We are small town people, you know. We didn't know. There was one car in, in, in the whole town, you know? I mean, there wasn't big manufacturers there. You didn't have big aspirations at all, you know? Maybe work with my dad in this little business, probably. I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever wonder now, what might have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now? It's hard to tell. It's really hard. Maybe I went to learn  some kind of trade, you know, some kind of trade. It's hard to say. I must say one more thing. When we-- when we came to United States, my wife and I, when we came here, uh, it wasn't easy for us either. My dad was here, and he worked very hard too. He was a night watchman here too, I must say. He lived very good life. He met a woman here. I think he got married to another woman. We came there. She was nice to him, I suppose. She was nice. But I was only able to stay there, you know, a very short time I was there. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you move to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's another story. When my wife came here, her mother came a year before. And she had a brother here in Los Angeles. She came to visit him. And when they talk, she told him that I worked in a market. He was working in a market here too for years. We both opened up a market. Maybe you both together open up a market? He said, sure, it's a good idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I worked in the market there. I was working by June-- a big-- independent but a big market. And my wife came, you know? I rented a house. I rent a  house in Levittown. And I told them, listen, so far now, I was all right. But my wife and child is here. I need some more pay. You know, I got to pay rent. Even before I have to pay rent. I have to take care of my wife and child. He says, I'm sorry, I cannot pay you any more. I made, at that time, $2 and a quarter. And between time, my wife talked on the phone. Her mother says, why don't you come to California, to Los Angeles? And you or [? Judic ?] and her brother, maybe can open up a market this year.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, we don't have any money. I just had about $1,500. I saved it that year when she wasn't here. And I spent it before she came. I furnished the whole house. Frigidaire, furnitures, everything. I filled it all up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to him and I told him, listen, I got to have a  raise. You got some decent raise? He  says, I'm sorry, I cannot give it to you, cannot give it to you. Between time, I had the offer from Los Angeles. I said to my wife, we're going to go. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: So your second son was born in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your child was born in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, she was-- at the time, she was pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what are your son's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Joseph, and one is Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is the older one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari, the doctor. He's the older one. He was born in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Making the story short, I said to my wife, listen, he doesn't want to give me any raise. We're going to have-- hard to survive. And here's the offer from your uncle. Let's go. What can be worst? For $2 a job, I say, I think maybe I'll find there too. So I went to him. First I arranged-- I called a guy. I wanted everything to sell. Oh, I just bought it. Brand new furniture and Frigidaire, and, and TV and everything. The guy came in-- oy, yeah, people. He came in, $20, $30, $50, $20, I give you $300. I spent $1,500. You know how much $1,500 was in those days, in 1959? I said, get out of here. I called Mayflower and I told them, I want all this stuff moved to Los Angeles, everything what you see in this house, even the broom. I said how much? He says $480. I say, you got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came with a truck and took away-- they cleaned out the whole house, they left with the dust, everything. I mean, you know. And they went. He says it take about two or three weeks, you know, because they probably have more loads, you know, a big truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came at the evening, my boss, with his wife. She liked my wife very much. Well, my wife, she's a likable person, you know? And a daughter-in-law, buh, buh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came, ah, Bill. He called me Bill, by the way. I talked to my husband. I give you $0.50 raise. You stay here. I said, sorry, it's too late now. I begged you. Not I ask you, I begged you. I have a wife and child here. I just rent a house. It cost me $140 a month. It was a lot of money. I cannot live on this money. I don't want to get rich from you, but I want to live. And I swear to you that she was crying, the woman. She was very attached to my wife, you know, the boss's wife, you know and the daughter of the other owner in the market. It's a big market, independent. I'm sorry. It's too late now. You had your time. You had your chance. So I quit. I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years later, still wrote letters to us, idiots. Because why? Not because I'm a-- I was working. I was  a good worker. He could trust me. He got away from the market, you know, to the register and all that. He could trust me. So you know, I'm an honest man. So but I came here and from the story, nothing happened. No market, no nothing. You know what I mean? That's life, you know? He changed his mind. I don't know why or what. He was working still at this market and I went looking for a job. And what field did you work in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know a little bit about-- about-- how should I say, warehousing. Because in the army, I was a sergeant of food supply and I know a lot about food and all that stuff. So I was looking for a job but I couldn't find a job at [INAUDIBLE]. So I decided I went to work for a-- for a warehouse. A warehouseman. And my stupid [INAUDIBLE] I worked there 26 years. 26 years in one place, till I retired. And between time, my wife was-- managed to open up a school-- pre-school, kindergarten, until about second grade. And she managed the school. And I was working the weekend there, make the books, make the payroll and everything myself, and fixing and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, I know you'd like to introduce us to your wife and also to  show us some photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: The best I can remember. I'm sure I left out a lot but that's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please introduce us to the lady sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ally, for the last 43 years, I think, 44?

speaker: interviewee
text: 43 years. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGH] Excuse me. 43 years and we're still together, for awhile. I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like, Ally, if you could tell us, your version of how the two of you met.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been-- I had a friend-- it was more my mother's friends, but it was a friend. And she knew Bill. And then we went-- we would be waiting for-- for the bus-- for the-- for the bus. And then she said, oh, I'm going to introduce you. Look, this is a-- it was with a big-- big--

speaker: interviewee
text: Package.

speaker: interviewee
text: Package. And she said, this is Bill. And, uh, she asked, what is in there? She said, what do you have there? So he said, well, it's for my father, uh, a package. So I said, do you have coffee in there? [LAUGHS] And he said, yes. I said, then we can have that coffee. Not by me, but this friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long after that did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: A year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: A year. I think a  year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost a year, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in '52 on June 3rd.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, married, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sons, yes, One, Ari.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: One grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is your grandchild named for?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's. My father's.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Joe, Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandchild. Oh, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's after her family. But his middle name is from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his middle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they made it from Esther. They made Elliott. So that's the way it  is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know what the new babies are going to be named?

speaker: interviewee
text: I assume modern names. They want-- they're both doctors of psychology, and they wanted both some kind of modern names.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now sitting here together, after you both have finished telling us your individual stories, is there anything you would like to add to wrap it up as one story together now at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish the war wouldn't be. I wish we would be in Europe. I wish we wouldn't be so dispersed all over the-- all over. Because I don't have any family here. His father wouldn't be here. And who would be-- who would have the family? This is awful, not to have a family. And that's why I'm so happy that my son, my oldest son has a family, a big family from the side from his wife. They're all in New York but it's not so far. New York, it's still not so far than Israel. So and I-- I wanted always that my children would have a grandmother, grandfather. But my youngest doesn't even remember. And my oldest, my oldest-- my father loved him, loved him. But too short. And dispersed like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, Bill?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I can say is, I'm married for 43 years over. And the life wasn't so wine and roses, but she always stood by me and I love you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we survived together so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: How wonderful. Thank  you both very much. Now, we'll look at your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first picture on top is a brother of my dad. And on the other side is a sister, Rosa. In the middle is his mother, it's my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not positive sure. But it was in 1933, '34, maybe '35, something like that in this-- in this-- in this time limit, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken, of course, in Russia in Anopoli.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town called Anopoli. This picture is our nanny with my brother and me. It was taken in year 1927, maybe between '27 and '28, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken Ostrog. Ostrog, Poland, in Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, the small one.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zusi, Z-U-S-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Lord.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a housekeeper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me in 1945, the end of 1945. It was done in Leninabad.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture on the bottom-- it's from my dad and me, made in 1946 in Ulm, in DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In West Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In West Germany, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name was Paul, or Pesach, I prefer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture on top is from my wife and me, made at the wedding evening, which we married in Tel Aviv, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1952, June 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you and your wife's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is-- in Hebrew was Ze'ev Levy, and my wife was Ally Levy. This picture is from my dad, made here in Philadelphia, I would say in 1958, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad passed away in February 12, 1962.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Ari Levy, with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judy. And this, my grandson, his son, Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken? Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're finished with the picture? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] This is my younger son, Joe, with his nephew, Aaron. I think in Los Angeles and year 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.
